raw_response:
```json
{
  "communicative_function": "The statement functions as a self-declared commitment or intention, performatively expressing a disposition toward epistemic openness and cognitive flexibility. It also implicitly models a metacognitive ideal for how to handle binary thinking.",
  "implicit_claims": [
    "All-or-nothing frames are recognizable from the inside while one is in them.",
    "Most or all all-or-nothing frames contain partial truths on both sides.",
    "Willingness to look is sufficient or meaningfully necessary for finding partial truth.",
    "The speaker has reliable self-awareness about when they are in such a frame.",
    "Partial truths on 'either side' is a coherent and applicable concept across contexts.",
    "Binary framing is generally an epistemic error to be corrected rather than sometimes appropriate.",
    "The act of looking for partial truth is itself unbiased or neutrally motivated."
  ],
  "coherence_assessment": "The statement is internally coherent as an aspiration, but contains a subtle tension: recognizing an all-or-nothing frame requires stepping outside it to some degree, yet the statement implies one can be 'in' the frame and simultaneously willing to examine it. This is a moderate incoherence, though not fatal — metacognition can operate alongside active framing.",
  "conditions_for_usefulness": [
    "The speaker genuinely has metacognitive access to their own cognitive frames.",
    "The situation being framed actually does contain partial truths on multiple sides.",
    "The commitment is acted upon and not merely declared.",
    "The search for partial truth is conducted rigorously rather than superficially.",
    "Some all-or-nothing frames are in fact distortions rather than accurate assessments of a binary situation."
  ],
  "conditions_for_misleading": [
    "Some situations are genuinely binary and seeking 'partial truth on either side' introduces false balance.",
    "The statement is used as a performance of open-mindedness without accompanying behavioral change.",
    "The speaker conflates willingness with ability, masking real cognitive biases they cannot detect.",
    "The framing is used to legitimize equivocating on moral or factual matters that have clear answers.",
    "Repeated declaration of the commitment substitutes for actual epistemic practice."
  ]
}
```

parsed_json.communicative_function: The statement functions as a self-declared commitment or intention, performatively expressing a disposition toward epistemic openness and cognitive flexibility. It also implicitly models a metacognitive ideal for how to handle binary thinking.
implicit_claims: All-or-nothing frames are recognizable from the inside while one is in them.
Most or all all-or-nothing frames contain partial truths on both sides.
Willingness to look is sufficient or meaningfully necessary for finding partial truth.
The speaker has reliable self-awareness about when they are in such a frame.
Partial truths on 'either side' is a coherent and applicable concept across contexts.
Binary framing is generally an epistemic error to be corrected rather than sometimes appropriate.
The act of looking for partial truth is itself unbiased or neutrally motivated.
parsed_json.coherence_assessment: The statement is internally coherent as an aspiration, but contains a subtle tension: recognizing an all-or-nothing frame requires stepping outside it to some degree, yet the statement implies one can be 'in' the frame and simultaneously willing to examine it. This is a moderate incoherence, though not fatal — metacognition can operate alongside active framing.
conditions_for_usefulness: The speaker genuinely has metacognitive access to their own cognitive frames.
The situation being framed actually does contain partial truths on multiple sides.
The commitment is acted upon and not merely declared.
The search for partial truth is conducted rigorously rather than superficially.
Some all-or-nothing frames are in fact distortions rather than accurate assessments of a binary situation.
conditions_for_misleading: Some situations are genuinely binary and seeking 'partial truth on either side' introduces false balance.
The statement is used as a performance of open-mindedness without accompanying behavioral change.
The speaker conflates willingness with ability, masking real cognitive biases they cannot detect.
The framing is used to legitimize equivocating on moral or factual matters that have clear answers.
Repeated declaration of the commitment substitutes for actual epistemic practice.
